questions.question: Explain the purpose and function of Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) in IPSec.
questions.hint: Focus on confidentiality, integrity, and authentication functions
keyPoints: ESP provides confidentiality through encryption
ESP provides integrity and authentication via ICV
ESP operates at the IP layer as part of IPSec suite
It protects the payload of IP packets
It can optionally provide anti-replay protection using sequence numbers

questions.question: Describe the fields in the ESP header format.
questions.hint: List all fields in the ESP header with their sizes and purposes
keyPoints: Security Parameters Index (SPI) - 32-bit field identifying SA
Sequence Number - 32-bit monotonically increasing counter
Payload Data - encrypted data
Padding - for block alignment
Pad Length - indicates padding length
Next Header - type of next header
Authentication Data - optional ICV for integrity

questions.question: What is the difference between ESP transport mode and ESP tunnel mode?
questions.hint: Compare the scope of encryption in both modes
keyPoints: Transport mode encrypts only the payload, keeping IP header visible
Tunnel mode encrypts the entire original IP packet including header
Transport mode used for host-to-host communication
Tunnel mode used for gateway-to-gateway or gateway-to-host
Tunnel mode creates new IP header for encrypted packet

questions.question: Explain the role of Security Parameters Index (SPI) in ESP.
questions.hint: Think of SPI as a pointer to the SA database entry
keyPoints: SPI is a 32-bit identifier
It uniquely identifies the Security Association (SA)
SPI is combined with destination IP address and protocol number
It helps receiver select correct SA for packet processing
SPI is present in both ESP header and used for lookup

questions.question: Why is padding required in ESP?
questions.hint: Consider block cipher requirements and traffic analysis protection
keyPoints: Encryption algorithms require input as multiple of block size
Padding ensures payload aligns to algorithm's block size
Padding also helps hide actual length of plaintext
Pad Length field indicates number of padding bytes
Common block sizes: 64-bit (DES), 128-bit (AES)

questions.question: Explain the ESP packet processing steps at the sender and receiver ends.
questions.hint: Break down into sender and receiver processing with proper sequence
keyPoints: Sender: Select appropriate SA based on destination
Sender: Add SPI and sequence number to header
Sender: Apply padding if required for encryption
Sender: Encrypt payload using chosen algorithm
Sender: Calculate and append authentication data (if confidentiality + auth selected)
Receiver: Extract SPI and locate SA
Receiver: Verify sequence number for anti-replay
Receiver: Verify authentication data first
Receiver: Decrypt payload and remove padding
Receiver: Pass decrypted packet to upper layer

questions.question: Compare ESP with AH (Authentication Header) protocol in terms of security services provided.
questions.hint: List security services provided by each protocol
keyPoints: AH provides only authentication and integrity, no confidentiality
ESP provides confidentiality (encryption) plus optional authentication
AH protects IP header fields that don't change in transit
ESP protects only the payload data
AH uses ICV calculation covering entire packet including immutable fields
ESP can use NULL encryption for authentication only
Both provide anti-replay protection via sequence numbers
ESP is more widely used due to confidentiality feature

questions.question: Discuss the encryption algorithms supported by ESP with their key sizes.
questions.hint: List major algorithms with their key sizes
keyPoints: DES (Data Encryption Standard) - 56-bit key
3DES (Triple DES) - 168-bit key (three 56-bit keys)
AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) - 128, 192, or 256-bit keys
NULL algorithm - no encryption (for authentication only mode)
RC5, IDEA, CAST are other optional algorithms
AES-GCM provides authenticated encryption
Choice affects security level and performance

questions.question: Describe the ESP trailer format and explain the Next Header field.
questions.hint: Structure your answer covering all trailer fields
keyPoints: ESP trailer contains: Padding, Pad Length, Next Header
Padding: 0-255 bytes for block alignment
Pad Length: 8-bit field indicating padding length
Next Header: 8-bit field identifies protocol of encrypted payload
Values: 4 (IP), 6 (TCP), 17 (UDP), 50 (ESP)
Authentication trailer follows if auth is selected

questions.question: Explain how ESP provides anti-replay protection using sequence numbers.
questions.hint: Focus on the sliding window mechanism at receiver
keyPoints: Each ESP packet has a unique 32-bit sequence number
Sequence number starts at 1 and increments for each packet
Sender increments sequence number for every packet sent
Receiver maintains a sliding window for accepted sequence numbers
Packets with reused sequence numbers are rejected
This prevents attackers from capturing and replaying packets
Window size is configurable, typically 64 packets

questions.question: Calculate the padding required for an ESP packet with a TCP payload of 300 bytes using AES-128 encryption (128-bit = 16 bytes block size).
questions.hint: Account for Next Header byte in the calculation
keyPoints: TCP payload = 300 bytes
Add Next Header field (1 byte) = 301 bytes
Block size for AES = 16 bytes
Find remainder: 301 mod 16 = 13
Padding needed = 16 - 13 = 3 bytes minimum
However, if result is 0, still add full block of 16 for plaintext length ambiguity
Total padded data = 301 + 3 = 304 bytes (divisible by 16)

questions.question: In an ESP tunnel mode operation, if the original IP packet is 1500 bytes and a new IP header of 20 bytes is added, calculate the total overhead. Given: SPI (4 bytes) + Sequence Number (4 bytes) + Authentication Tag (12 bytes) + AES block padding.
questions.hint: Consider original IP header is also encrypted in tunnel mode
keyPoints: Original packet = 1500 bytes
New IP header = 20 bytes (outer header)
ESP header = SPI + Seq No = 4 + 4 = 8 bytes
Authentication tag = 12 bytes
For AES-128: block size = 16 bytes
Payload = 1500 + 20 (original IP header) + Next Header (1) = 1521 bytes
Padding: 1521 mod 16 = 1, so need 15 bytes padding
Pad Length field = 1 byte
Total overhead = 20 + 8 + 15 + 1 + 12 = 56 bytes

questions.question: Design an ESP Security Association (SA) entry with following specifications: AES-256 for encryption, HMAC-SHA-256 for authentication, Transport mode, and anti-replay window of 64 packets. List all fields that would be in this SA database entry.
questions.hint: Include both security parameters and operational parameters
keyPoints: Destination IP Address
SPI (Security Parameters Index) - unique 32-bit value
Encryption Algorithm: AES-256
Encryption Key: 256-bit (32 bytes) key material
Authentication Algorithm: HMAC-SHA-256
Authentication Key: 256-bit key material
Mode: Transport
Sequence Number Counter: Initial value (typically 1)
Anti-replay Window: 64 packets (bitmap of 64 bits)
Lifetime: Expiry time or byte count
IPSec Protocol: ESP (protocol number 50)

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Next Header field in ESP?
questions.hint: Similar to Protocol field in IP header
keyPoints: It indicates the protocol type of the encapsulated payload
Values follow Internet Protocol numbers
Value 6 indicates TCP
Value 17 indicates UDP
Value 4 indicates IP (for nested tunnel)
Value 50 indicates ESP itself
Helps demultiplex at receiver to pass to correct protocol handler

questions.question: Explain the concept of ESP combined mode and its advantages.
questions.hint: Consider algorithms like AES-GCM that provide AEAD
keyPoints: Combined mode uses algorithms that provide both encryption and authentication
Examples: AES-GCM, AES-CCM
Single algorithm performs both functions efficiently
Advantages: Reduced computational overhead
Advantages: Smaller packet overhead (single pass processing)
Advantages: Better security through integrated design
Authentication covers encrypted data (Encrypt-then-MAC)
